common_name: Emerald Blueberry (UF Southern Highbush Excellent-Flavor Low-Chill Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root plants in late fall to early spring in well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun; Emerald requires cross-pollination from another southern highbush variety (Jewel, Southmoon, O'Neal) for maximum fruit production — plant two or more compatible varieties within 50 feet.

directions: Plant in well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun; Emerald is a University of Florida IFAS southern highbush blueberry introduction recognized as one of the finest-flavored and most productive low-chill blueberry varieties for Gulf South gardens — producing large, round, sky-blue to medium-blue berries with a desirable balance of sweetness and mild acidity, firm texture, small dry scar, and excellent fresh-eating quality that compares favorably with the best northern highbush varieties. Emerald requires only approximately 200 chill hours (hours below 45°F) for adequate dormancy break and reliable annual fruit production — far fewer than the 700–1,000+ hours required by northern highbush varieties, making it fully adapted to zone 9 winters where chill accumulation frequently falls in the 200–400 hour range. The early to mid-season ripening (March–May in zone 9) provides fresh local blueberries before summer heat arrives. Soil pH management is the most critical cultural requirement: blueberries in general, and southern highbush especially, require very acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) — outside this range, nutrients become unavailable regardless of fertilization, causing slow growth, yellowing foliage, and poor production. Amend soil with sulfur well before planting to lower pH; maintain with acidifying fertilizer (azalea/camellia formulas) and pine bark mulch. For maximum production, plant Emerald with at least one other southern highbush variety for cross-pollination.

common_name: Jewel Blueberry (UF Very-Early Southern Highbush Low-Chill Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root plants in late fall to early spring in well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun; Jewel's very early ripening season makes it particularly valuable for commercial operations and home gardeners who want the season's first fresh blueberries — it ripens 1–2 weeks ahead of most other southern highbush varieties.

directions: Plant in well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun; Jewel is a University of Florida IFAS very-early-ripening southern highbush blueberry that starts production 1–2 weeks ahead of Emerald and most other Gulf South varieties — providing the season's first fresh blueberries in February to March in zone 9, making Jewel particularly valuable for extending the blueberry harvest season when planted with later-ripening varieties. The medium-large, blue berries have good flavor and firm texture with a small dry scar — the fresh-eating quality is good for a very early variety, though some tasters find the peak flavor of mid-season varieties like Emerald and O'Neal slightly superior. Jewel's very low chill hour requirement (approximately 150–200 hours) makes it one of the best-adapted blueberries for the warmest zone 9 locations where winters are mild and chill accumulation is uncertain. Jewel is upright and moderately vigorous, reaching 4–5 feet with regular management. As with all southern highbush blueberries, very acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) is the fundamental requirement — plant in raised beds or containers with acidic media (50% pine bark plus peat) if native soil pH cannot be sufficiently lowered. Plant with Emerald, Southmoon, or O'Neal for cross-pollination that significantly increases berry size and total yield.

common_name: Southmoon Blueberry (UF Mid-Season Large-Berry Southern Highbush Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root plants in late fall to early spring in well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun; Southmoon can tolerate higher pH (up to 6.0) compared to other highbush blueberries — a practical advantage in Gulf South soils where achieving pH below 5.0 can be challenging.

directions: Plant in well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun; Southmoon is a University of Florida IFAS southern highbush blueberry introduction valued for its large berry size, excellent flavor, higher-pH tolerance than most highbush varieties, and vigorous upright growth habit that makes it one of the most productive and commercially successful southern highbush varieties. The large, light-blue to sky-blue berries are firm with a small, dry picking scar — the combination of large size, good flavor, and excellent firmness makes Southmoon outstanding for both fresh market and home garden production. Southmoon's relative tolerance for soil pH up to 6.0 (compared to the strict 4.5–5.5 requirement of most blueberries) is practically significant for Gulf South gardeners who have difficulty reducing clay soil pH — Southmoon performs in slightly less-acidic conditions where other varieties would show severe nutrient deficiency. Southmoon is mid-season (April–May in zone 9), producing its main crop after the early varieties (Jewel, Emerald) and before the latest-ripening types, making it valuable for extending the blueberry season as part of a multiple-variety planting. The vigorous, upright habit reaches 6–7 feet and benefits from annual post-harvest pruning to maintain productive, manageable size. A primary recommendation from University of Florida extension for Gulf South blueberry production.

common_name: Rosy Rain Lily (Cuban Pink Miniature Fall Rain Lily)

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Zephyranthes rosea naturalizes freely in Gulf South gardens, multiplying rapidly by offsets and self-seeding — plant in masses of 25+ bulbs for best display and allow to spread into expanding naturalized colonies.

directions: Plant bulbs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Zephyranthes rosea (Rosy Rain Lily or Cuban Rain Lily) is a small but charming fall-blooming bulb native to Cuba and Guatemala that has naturalized widely through the Gulf South as a garden escape — producing small (1.5–2 inch diameter), funnel-shaped, rose-pink to warm-pink flowers on 6–8 inch stems in flushes triggered by rainfall events from late summer through fall. The delightful blooming response to rainfall — a period of dry weather followed by a good rain triggers near-simultaneous flowering across an established colony within 2–3 days — creates the characteristic rain lily display that is one of the most charming garden phenomena of the Gulf South fall season. Zephyranthes rosea is the smallest and most miniature of the commonly grown rain lily species, with flowers somewhat smaller than Z. grandiflora but produced in great profusion on well-established colonies. The warm rose-pink color is more saturated and vivid than the pale shell-pink of Z. grandiflora — a clear, definite pink that reads well at a distance in naturalized mass plantings. Established colonies multiply rapidly by bulb offsets and prolific self-seeding, eventually forming dense patches that provide reliable annual display with no maintenance. Outstanding for naturalizing along lawn edges, in rock gardens, under deciduous trees, and along garden paths where the self-seeding habit can fill in naturally.

common_name: Red Fountain Grass (Burgundy-Purple Tropical Ornamental Grass)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun; 'Rubrum' is a sterile cultivar — it does not set viable seed, unlike the invasive straight species Pennisetum setaceum, making it safe to plant without concern for naturalization.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun; Red Fountain Grass ('Rubrum') is one of the most visually dramatic warm-season ornamental grasses for Gulf South gardens — producing mounding, arching clumps of narrow, burgundy-purple to dark wine-red leaves topped by reddish-bronze to copper-colored bottle-brush flower plumes in summer and fall that create an elegant, soft-textured, colorful mass that stands out brilliantly against green or silver companions. The foliage color — maintained throughout the growing season from spring through fall rather than only at bloom time — provides a sustained burgundy accent that few other ornamental grasses offer. The 'Rubrum' cultivar is sterile and does not set viable seed, making it non-invasive despite the parent species (Pennisetum setaceum) being a serious invasive in some warm climates (Hawaii, California) — the sterility of 'Rubrum' is an important ecological distinction. In zone 9, Red Fountain Grass is reliably perennial, dying back in cold winters but returning from the roots in spring; in zone 9b and 10, it may remain semi-evergreen through mild winters. Drought-tolerant once established and excellent in full-sun, reflected-heat positions that challenge many ornamental grasses. Outstanding in containers, as a specimen accent, in mixed borders, and in combination with silver companions (dusty miller, Powis Castle artemisia) where the burgundy-red and silver combination is striking.
